title: Power

canonical.invariant: Power is effective capacity to produce outcomes in relation to a governance structure.
excludes: accepted or justified validity of rule or standing

adjacent.authority: Authority concerns recognized standing to direct, not effective capacity by itself.
adjacent.legitimacy: Legitimacy concerns valid standing within an order, not operative capacity to produce outcomes.

shortDefinition: Power is effective capacity to produce outcomes in relation to a governance structure.
coreMeaning: Within the governance domain, power functions through the ability to produce effects, secure compliance, or alter conditions in relation to a governance structure. It does not depend on recognized right or accepted validity.

structuralProfile.function: effective capacity to produce outcomes in relation to governance
structuralProfile.object: outcomes, compliance, or altered conditions within a governance structure
structuralProfile.sourceType: resources, coercion, institutional position, coordination, or control
structuralProfile.actorRelation: actor or office holds operative capacity to affect others or the order
structuralProfile.enforcementRole: can support enforcement capacity without being enforcement validity
structuralProfile.outcomeAttributionRole: does not attribute outcomes normatively; it produces or shapes them

invariants.statement: Power is operative capacity to produce outcomes in relation to governance.
Power is not recognized directive standing.
Power is not accepted or justified validity.
Power can operate without recognized authority.

invariantBreaches.condition: A power claim assigns power a non-capacity identity within the governance domain.
invariantBreaches.reason: Power must remain operative outcome capacity.

invariantBreaches.condition: A power claim treats power as recognized directive standing or as equivalent to authority.
invariantBreaches.reason: Power must not collapse into authority.

invariantBreaches.condition: A power claim treats power as justified validity or as equivalent to legitimacy.
invariantBreaches.reason: Power must not collapse into legitimacy.

refusals.condition: The requested usage is outside the governance domain.
refusals.reason: Power is locked to governance-domain meaning in ChatPDM v1.

refusals.condition: The requested usage is listed as out of scope in the governance domain lock.
refusals.reason: The governance domain lock excludes that topic from the power contract.

refusals.condition: The query asks power to stand in a relation not admitted by the power contract.
refusals.reason: This power contract admits only operative-capacity relations inside the governance cluster.

contractIncompletes.condition: The power constraint contract is missing a required structural element or cannot be resolved for validation.
contractIncompletes.reason: Power must expose a complete operative-capacity contract before admission or runtime use.

runtimeBoundaries.condition: A power packet or power-like claim is referenced for runtime use without live admission.
runtimeBoundaries.reason: Only live-admitted power may participate in runtime matching or comparison.

valid: All required fields are present and non-empty.
If relationType is present, the relation-target pair is listed in allowedRelations.
invalid: A required field is missing or empty.
conflict: The claim assigns power an identity other than operative outcome capacity.
The claim collapses power into authority or legitimacy.
The claim asserts a forbidden equivalent_to relation.
refused: The requested usage is outside the governance domain lock.
The requested topic is explicitly out of scope.
The requested relation is not admitted by the power contract.

fullDefinition:
Power is not identical to authority or legitimacy. In ChatPDM v1 it is defined within the governance domain as effective capacity to produce outcomes in relation to a governance structure.

Its internal composition may rest in resources, coercive capacity, institutional position, coordination, or control over dependencies. The concept therefore concerns what can be made to happen, not who may rightfully direct or whether that capacity counts as valid.

Within governance structures, power explains operative effect. Authority may channel it and legitimacy may justify it, but neither is required for power to exist or to work.

standard.shortDefinition: Power is effective capacity to produce outcomes in relation to a governance structure.
standard.coreMeaning: Within the governance domain, power functions through the ability to produce effects, secure compliance, or alter conditions in relation to a governance structure. It does not depend on recognized right or accepted validity.
standard.fullDefinition:
Power is not identical to authority or legitimacy. In ChatPDM v1 it is defined within the governance domain as effective capacity to produce outcomes in relation to a governance structure.

Its internal composition may rest in resources, coercive capacity, institutional position, coordination, or control over dependencies. The concept therefore concerns what can be made to happen, not who may rightfully direct or whether that capacity counts as valid.

Within governance structures, power explains operative effect. Authority may channel it and legitimacy may justify it, but neither is required for power to exist or to work.

simplified.shortDefinition: Power means being able to make outcomes happen in a governance structure.
simplified.coreMeaning: In governance, power is the ability to produce effects, gain compliance, or change conditions. It does not require a recognized right to rule or a valid claim to rule.
simplified.fullDefinition:
Power is not the same as authority or legitimacy. It is about what can actually be done in a governance structure.

Power can come from force, resources, position, coordination, or control over dependence. Those sources let actors shape outcomes even when no recognized right is present.

So power tracks effective impact. Authority asks who may direct. Legitimacy asks whether rule or standing is valid.

formal.shortDefinition: Power is the effective capacity by which outcomes are produced or conditions are altered within a governance structure.
formal.coreMeaning: Within the governance domain, power concerns the capacity to secure compliance, alter distributions, or determine effects in relation to an order of rule. It does not require recognized authority, nor does it depend on legitimacy as a condition of existence.
formal.fullDefinition: Power is defined, within the governance domain, as effective capacity to produce outcomes in relation to a governance structure. The capacity may arise from coercive means, resource control, institutional position, coordinated action, or dependency management, and it therefore concerns operative effect rather than recognized right. Authority may organize or channel power, and legitimacy may justify its exercise, but neither relation is constitutive of power itself.

holder.label: Actor able to secure effects within a governance structure

capability.description: capacity to produce outcomes, secure compliance, or alter conditions within governance

contexts.label: governance
contexts.explanation: Power concerns effective capacity to shape collective outcomes, compliance, or distribution in relation to governing order.

contexts.label: institutional
contexts.explanation: Power concerns control over decisions, dependencies, or resources inside organized structures.

contexts.label: coercive
contexts.explanation: Power concerns the ability to compel or shape outcomes even when recognized standing is absent or contested.

sources.label: Steven Lukes, Power: A Radical View
sources.usedFor: structural grounding for power as effective capacity rather than formal right

sources.label: Oxford Languages
sources.usedFor: definition boundary for capacity and ability to act or affect

authority.boundaryStatement: Power concerns effective capacity to produce outcomes, whereas authority concerns recognized standing to direct, decide, or govern within the order.
authority.nonSubstitutionRule: Do not substitute power for authority when the issue is recognized directive right rather than operative capacity to secure compliance or alter conditions.
authority.failureIfCollapsed: If power collapses into authority, the system would mistake coercive or resource-backed capacity for recognized right and would lose cases where power operates without authorized standing.
authority.validSeparationExample: A military commander may hold enough force to determine events even when the commander lacks the authority to issue a lawful directive in that setting.
authority.invalidCollapseExample: Treating any actor who can compel obedience as having authority collapses power into authority.

legitimacy.boundaryStatement: Power concerns operative capacity to produce outcomes, whereas legitimacy concerns whether rule, office, or standing counts as accepted or justified within the order.
legitimacy.nonSubstitutionRule: Do not substitute power for legitimacy when the question is about valid standing or accepted rule rather than about effective control, leverage, or compliance production.
legitimacy.failureIfCollapsed: If power collapses into legitimacy, the system would treat sheer effectiveness as sufficient validity and would erase cases where force works even though the order is rejected.
legitimacy.validSeparationExample: A regime can retain power through coercive control after its legitimacy has broken down among the governed.
legitimacy.invalidCollapseExample: Calling a government legitimate simply because it can enforce its orders collapses power into legitimacy.
